focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Rain had started before daylight and never quite grown into a storm. It came down in the mean, steady way that soaked everything without giving anybody the decency of thunder. By the time Della reached the lower gate of Old Sion, the half-mile path from Cedar Bench had turned to a dragged strip of red grease, and the hems of her dress were heavy as feed sacks. Water ran in little brown threads between the graves. The cedar roots held where they could and let go where they could not. Down in the fever plot the slope looked wrong in a way she felt first in her knees, before she could have named it with words. The earth there had lost its patience. It bellied outward under the grass as if something under it had taken a long breath.

She stood a moment with one hand on the gatepost and made herself take air slowly. Her chest had been shorter with her lately. Not sick shorter, not the hot tightness of winter cough, but a workman's shortage, as if every hill had grown steeper in the night and meant to stay that way. Her left thumb had locked around the tin pail handle on the climb. She had to pry it open with the fingers of the other hand before she could lift the latch.

Silas Wynn was already inside the gate, setting a plank down near the wagon track with the care of a man lowering something breakable though the plank was only wet pine. He had tied a strip of worn leather around the handle of his shovel where his palm rubbed raw, and he still had his cough rag tucked neat into his breast pocket instead of balled damp in a trouser leg like most men would. He glanced at her and then at the hill below them.

Della took one look at the plank and felt a mean little chill walk her spine. Silas did not waste dry wood on worry. If he was laying a path before the hands...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ions of one another's lives home in tins and jars. June was by the sideboard with Pearline, copying names from the bottoms of two cake plates onto a scrap of paper because the pencil marks had smeared.

"Miss Doss says Larkin High's got commercial classes," June was saying. "Typing and bookkeeping both."

Pearline, stacking minute books into a box that smelled of cedar and mold, said, "If your folks can spare you the time."

June glanced up then and saw Della watching. She did not look ashamed of what she had asked. She looked resolved in a new, careful way, as if a room full of adults failing one another had taught her something she meant to make use of.

Beyond her, Vernon stood by the church door shaking hands with men who wanted his approval more than they wanted peace. Noreen had wrapped two pieces of chess pie in wax paper for later and was talking to Mrs. Fairley about rent in town and where a body might still find sorrel close enough to gather. Miss Cora was arguing with Olan over whose son had promised to bring cedar saplings to Limestone Ridge first. Ruthie was wringing out a cloth. Rafe had gone to hauling chairs again, working hard enough at it to make labor look like a verdict.

Della dried the platter until the painted roses on its rim came clear under the film of grease. Around her the room had gone back to ordinary voices, but she could hear the quieter work going on underneath: people placing one another, deciding which story each body belonged to. The hearing would not be a legal errand in Larkin. It would be this room carried into county light. Whatever name she put to the fever-plot dead would not stay on the dead alone. It would fasten itself to the living: thief, bastard, victim, witness. The water was still downriver. The naming had already begun.
